RBIF 108: Week 9 Assignment

Jonathan Hummel
2025-05-31
Table of Contents
1) The MAPK model CytosolicMAPK.m RunMAPKModel.m 3) Exploring the model 3) References

1) The MAPK model

Below is the function I put together that contains the system of ODEs for the cytosolic MAPK reactions formulated by Tian and Song [1]. To avoid errors while manually copying the provided differential equations, I used SimBiology to enter the first 9 lines of chemical reactions defined in Section 1 of the Supplemental Information in a single compartment model (i.e. the cytosol). This allowed me to set up the rate constants using the same conventions followed in the paper and supplementary parameters table, as well as establish consistent and MATLAB-friendly variable naming for complexes. I copied the resulting differential equations from SimBiology and then used a text editor and a few regex substitutions to format the equations for MATLAB and remove unneccesary volume terms. This approach put some guardrails in place to prevent typos. The reactions that were included in this system cover those involving only species in the cytosol. As such, this model does not account for any transport to/from the nucelus, or the affect of reactions in the nucleus on the species accounted for. Each of the 9 lines of reactions used from the supplemental information include 2 reactions: the reversible binding of an enzyme and substrate to create a complex (with association and dissociation rate constants 'a' and 'd'), followed by irreversible formation of a product and release of the enzyme (with rate constant 'k' ). Those "irreversible" second steps are often effectively reversed by other enzyme-catalyzed reactions in the system (e.g., compare first and second reactions in Supplemental Section 1). The diagram below, taken from the paper, provides an overview of the reactions included [1]:
Diagram of reactions expressed in "CytosolicMAPK.m", taken from Figure 1 by Tian and Song [1].
As seen above, the concentration of Ras-GTP (simply "Ras" in the model) is the activating input signal to this cascade. Upstream of the processes modeled here, Ras-GTP would start being generated at the cytosolic side of the cell memrane via activated EGFR (a receptor tyrosine kinase) and/or its associated proteins. This is all initially triggered by EGFR binding with its ligand, EGF [2]. In this model, Ras-GTP kicks off a series of phosphorylations, in the presence of counterbalancing dephosphorylations (catalyzed by the respective phosphatases). Raf and MEK are the kinase intermediaries in this cascade. The system expressed in the code below includes 21 equations because, as implied by the diagram, there are numerous complexes between the species explicitly depicted above. This cascade of reactions ultimately leads to the generation of dual phosphorylated ERK (ERKpp). While the biological reality may be more complex and context-specific, dual phosphorylation of ERK is neccesary for proper catalytic activity, so for the sake of this model and discussion, ERKpp is considered the final output (i.e., the "ON" signal to anything downstream) [3,4]. Outside of the scope of this model, ERKpp would then drive other activity elsewhere (e.g., expression changes in the nucleus) [1,2].

CytosolicMAPK.m

function dXdt = CytosolicMAPK(t, x, p, controlRas, RasFunction)
% CytosolicMAPK: A model of MAPK signaling in the cytosol. Model reactions
% and parameters were taken from Tian & Song (2012). DOI:10.1371/journal.pone.0042230
% Only cytosolic reactions in the Supplementary Information (Section 1) were used.
% 'x' holds the concentrations/states of species (see their names below)
% 'p' is expected to be a table (or struct) containing columns (or fields) named after each
% required parameter (see below for which ones are required and how they should be named)
% 't' is time and is used by the ODE solvers
% Set 'controlRas' to true and pass a function of 't' as 'RasFunction'
% to modulate Ras concentration and see the MAPK system's response.
% 'RasFunction' should not ever drop below 0 or the system breaks!
% Ensure parameters fit the notation used in the system below
a1 = p.a1;
a2 = p.a2;
a3 = p.a3;
a4 = p.a4;
a5 = p.a5;
a6 = p.a6;
a7 = p.a7;
a8 = p.a8;
a9 = p.a9;
d1 = p.d1;
d2 = p.d2;
d3 = p.d3;
d4 = p.d4;
d5 = p.d5;
d6 = p.d6;
d7 = p.d7;
d8 = p.d8;
d9 = p.d9;
k1 = p.k1;
k2 = p.k2;
k3 = p.k3;
k4 = p.k4;
k5 = p.k5;
k6 = p.k6;
k7 = p.k7;
k8 = p.k8;
k9 = p.k9;
 
% For clarity, define the species names as Tian & Song did
if (controlRas == true)
Ras = RasFunction(t); % Enables specification of [Ras] as an f(t)
else
Ras = x(1);
end
Raf = x(2);
Ras_Raf = x(3);
RafStar = x(4);
RafPase = x(5);
RafStar_RafPase = x(6);
MEK = x(7);
RafStar_MEK = x(8);
MEKpp = x(9);
MEKp = x(10);
MEKPase = x(11);
MEKp_MEKPase = x(12);
MEKpp_MEKPase = x(13);
ERK = x(14);
MEKpp_ERK = x(15);
ERKp = x(16);
MEKpp_ERKp = x(17);
ERKpp = x(18);
ERKPase = x(19);
ERKp_ERKPase = x(20);
ERKpp_ERKPase = x(21);
 
% Cytosolic ODE system, based on reaction lines 1 through 9 in Section 1
% in the Supplementary Information (Tian & Song, 2012).
% These equations were created by entering the reactions into
% SymBiology and copying + regex reformatting the resulting equations
if (controlRas ~= true) % If we are NOT modulating Ras concentration
dXdt(1) = -(a1*Ras*Raf-d1*Ras_Raf) + (k1*Ras_Raf); % d(Ras)/dt
end
dXdt(2) = -(a1*Ras*Raf-d1*Ras_Raf) + (k2*RafStar_RafPase); % d(Raf)/dt
dXdt(3) = (a1*Ras*Raf-d1*Ras_Raf) - (k1*Ras_Raf); % d(Ras_Raf)/dt
dXdt(4) = (k1*Ras_Raf) - (a2*RafStar*RafPase-d2*RafStar_RafPase) - (a3*RafStar*MEK-d3*RafStar_MEK) + (k3*RafStar_MEK); % d(RafStar)/dt
dXdt(5) = -(a2*RafStar*RafPase-d2*RafStar_RafPase) + (k2*RafStar_RafPase); % d(RafPase)/dt
dXdt(6) = (a2*RafStar*RafPase-d2*RafStar_RafPase) - (k2*RafStar_RafPase); % d(RafStar_RafPase)/dt
dXdt(7) = -(a3*RafStar*MEK-d3*RafStar_MEK) + (k4*MEKp_MEKPase); % d(MEK)/dt
dXdt(8) = (a3*RafStar*MEK-d3*RafStar_MEK) - (k3*RafStar_MEK); % d(RafStar_MEK)/dt
dXdt(9) = (k3*RafStar_MEK) - (a5*MEKpp*MEKPase-d5*MEKpp_MEKPase) - (a6*MEKpp*ERK-d6*MEKpp_ERK) + (k6*MEKpp_ERK) - (a7*MEKpp*ERKp-d7*MEKpp_ERKp) + (k7*MEKpp_ERKp); % d(MEKpp)/dt
dXdt(10) = -(a4*MEKp*MEKPase-d4*MEKp_MEKPase) + (k5*MEKpp_MEKPase); % d(MEKp)/dt
dXdt(11) = -(a4*MEKp*MEKPase-d4*MEKp_MEKPase) + (k4*MEKp_MEKPase) - (a5*MEKpp*MEKPase-d5*MEKpp_MEKPase) + (k5*MEKpp_MEKPase); % d(MEKPase)/dt
dXdt(12) = (a4*MEKp*MEKPase-d4*MEKp_MEKPase) - (k4*MEKp_MEKPase); % d(MEKp_MEKPase)/dt
dXdt(13) = (a5*MEKpp*MEKPase-d5*MEKpp_MEKPase) - (k5*MEKpp_MEKPase); % d(MEKpp_MEKPase)/dt
dXdt(14) = -(a6*MEKpp*ERK-d6*MEKpp_ERK) + (k8*ERKp_ERKPase); % d(ERK)/dt
dXdt(15) = (a6*MEKpp*ERK-d6*MEKpp_ERK) - (k6*MEKpp_ERK); % d(MEKpp_ERK)/dt
dXdt(16) = (k6*MEKpp_ERK) - (a7*MEKpp*ERKp-d7*MEKpp_ERKp) - (a8*ERKp*ERKPase-d8*ERKp_ERKPase) + (k9*ERKpp_ERKPase); % d(ERKp)/dt
dXdt(17) = (a7*MEKpp*ERKp-d7*MEKpp_ERKp) - (k7*MEKpp_ERKp); % d(MEKpp_ERKp)/dt
dXdt(18) = (k7*MEKpp_ERKp) - (a9*ERKpp*ERKPase-d9*ERKpp_ERKPase); % d(ERKpp)/dt
dXdt(19) = -(a8*ERKp*ERKPase-d8*ERKp_ERKPase) + (k8*ERKp_ERKPase) - (a9*ERKpp*ERKPase-d9*ERKpp_ERKPase) + (k9*ERKpp_ERKPase); % d(ERKPase)/dt
dXdt(20) = (a8*ERKp*ERKPase-d8*ERKp_ERKPase) - (k8*ERKp_ERKPase); % d(ERKp_ERKPase)/dt
dXdt(21) = (a9*ERKpp*ERKPase-d9*ERKpp_ERKPase) - (k9*ERKpp_ERKPase); % d(ERKpp_ERKPase)/dt
dXdt = dXdt';
end
Below is the code I wrote to solve the model defined above and plot the results for various Ras-GTP concentration scenarios. This code is based on various examples and assigments from past weeks. It is worth noting that, because [Ras] is technically a system/model input, I wrote the function above to enable passing an arbitrary function of time to be used to control Ras (instead of just leaving it at the flat line solution obtained otherwise). This feature is used repeatedly below to explore the system's behavior a bit. All code from the script is shown below, but I will display and discuss sets of the figures generated by it one at a time later on. It is worth noting that I chose to use the parameters and initial conditions from the paper by Tian and Song for the model fitted to absolute concentrations (referred to as "System 2" in the paper, Table 1, and Supplementary Table 1) [1].
NOTE: To facillitate automatic parameter assignment, I copy/pasted the information from the provided table of parameters (Supplementary Table 1) into MS Excel and exported a CSV (included with the scripts in my submission). This enabled those parameters to be easily imported into a MATLAB table, which was then passed as an argument to the CytosolicMAPK function defined above. In the process of inspecting the parameters, I noticed the following typos in the required parameters: "k6" was repeated twice in the parameter name column, "k8" was missing, and one parameter (presumably "k12") broke the naming convetion established in the rest of the paper ("l12") [1]. I resolved these issues by renaming all parameters after the first instance of "k6" using a consistent format and ascening integer order (i.e., "k7", "k8", "k9"...,"k15", see submitted CSV file). This resolution seemed to fit the most likely interpretation of what went wrong.

RunMAPKModel.m

%% Modeling using the system fitted to *absolute* protein concentrations
% AKA "System 2", as defined by Tian & Song (2012)
close all
 
%% Extract the (absolute) model parameters from a CSV created
% from Table S1 by Tian & Song (2012) as a table with columns as variables
paramsTable = readtable("./Tian_Song_MAPK_params.csv");
paramsTable = rows2vars(paramsTable,'VariableNamesSource', "ParamName",'DataVariables', {'System2_Absolute'});
 
%% Define the initial conditions as per Table 1 by Tian & Song (2012)
% concentrations not specified in the Table 1 were said to be zero
Ras0 = 0.4;
Raf0 = 0.013;
Ras_Raf0 = 0;
RafStar0 = 0;
RafPase0 = 0.002;
RafStar_RafPase0 = 0;
MEK0 = 1.4;
RafStar_MEK0 = 0;
MEKpp0 = 0;
MEKp0 = 0;
MEKPase0 = 0.14;
MEKp_MEKPase0 = 0;
MEKpp_MEKPase0 = 0;
ERK0 = 0.96;
MEKpp_ERK0 = 0;
ERKp0 = 0;
MEKpp_ERKp0 = 0;
ERKpp0 = 0;
ERKPase0 = 0.48;
ERKp_ERKPase0 = 0;
ERKpp_ERKPase0 = 0;
IC = [Ras0, Raf0, Ras_Raf0, RafStar0, RafPase0, RafStar_RafPase0, MEK0, RafStar_MEK0, MEKpp0, MEKp0, MEKPase0, MEKp_MEKPase0, MEKpp_MEKPase0, ERK0, MEKpp_ERK0, ERKp0, MEKpp_ERKp0, ERKpp0, ERKPase0, ERKp_ERKPase0, ERKpp_ERKPase0];
SpeciesNames = ["Ras", "Raf", "Ras_Raf", "RafStar", "RafPase", "RafStar_RafPase", "MEK", "RafStar_MEK", "MEKpp", "MEKp", "MEKPase", "MEKp_MEKPase", "MEKpp_MEKPase", "ERK", "MEKpp_ERK", "ERKp", "MEKpp_ERKp", "ERKpp", "ERKPase", "ERKp_ERKPase", "ERKpp_ERKPase"];
 
%% Set the convergence tolerance constraints for the ODE solver
options=odeset;
options.AbsTol=1e-6;
options.RelTol=1e-6;
%% Anonymize the function call, and assign the parameter set to this anonymized function
f=@(t,x)CytosolicMAPK(t,x,paramsTable,false);
 
%% Call the numerical ODE solver to solve the cytsolic MAPK system
% for initial conditions from Table 1 by Tian & Song (2012) (Ras0 = 0.4)
Trange = [0 30];
[T, X] = ode15s(f, Trange, IC, options);
Xtbl = array2table(X, 'VariableNames', SpeciesNames);
Xtbl.time = T;
 
%% Local convinience function to facillilate repeated plotting of states
function plotResults(dataTable, timeRange, concRange, selectedSpecies, plotTitle, paletteFxn, lineCycling)
figure;
plot(dataTable.time, dataTable{:,selectedSpecies}, 'LineWidth', 1)
colororder(paletteFxn(length(selectedSpecies)));
set(gca,'FontSize',12, 'LineStyleOrder',["-","--",":","-."], 'LineStyleCyclingMethod', lineCycling);
title(plotTitle, FontSize=14);
ylabel('Concentration (μM)', FontSize=13);
xlabel('Time (minutes)', FontSize=13);
xlim(timeRange);
ylim(concRange);
legend(selectedSpecies, 'Interpreter', 'none');
end
 
%% A spot check of the system's conservation of mass never hurts
massBalance = @(table, speciesCHAR) sum(table{:, strncmp(table.Properties.VariableNames, speciesCHAR, length(speciesCHAR))},2) - table{:,strcat(speciesCHAR, "Pase")}; % (SpeciesName)Pase is not a (SpeciesName) species!
time = T;
BalanceTable = table(T, massBalance(Xtbl, 'Raf'), massBalance(Xtbl, 'MEK'), massBalance(Xtbl, 'ERK'), ...
'VariableNames', ["time", "Raf_balance" "MEK_balance", "ERK_balance"]);
plotResults(BalanceTable, [0 30], [0 1.5], ["Raf_balance", "MEK_balance", "ERK_balance"], ...
'Mass balance spot check (Raf0 = 0.013, MEK0 = 1.4, ERK0 = 0.96)', @lines, 'aftercolor');
 
%% Plot solution for all species defined in the system
plotResults(Xtbl, [0 5], [0 1.5], SpeciesNames, ...
'Whole system response to Ras-GTP concentration of 0.4 μM', @hsv, 'withcolor');
 
%% Plot solution for only the initial (Ras) and final (ERK) species
% Response of ERKpp to Ras captures the overall signal transduction
plotResults(Xtbl, [0 5], [0 1], ["Ras", "ERK", "ERKp", "ERKpp"], ...
'ERK response to Ras-GTP constant at 0.4 μM', @lines, 'aftercolor');
 
 
%% Explore the sensitivity of ERK activation to different [Ras] behaviors
% Define some functions for Ras-GTP/input signal concentration
 
% Instantaneous step up and down in Ras-GTP
function rs = RasStep(t, tStepUp, tStepDown, loConc, highConc)
if (t < tStepUp)
rs = loConc;
elseif (t >= tStepUp && t < tStepDown)
rs = highConc;
elseif (t >= tStepDown)
rs = loConc;
end
end
 
% Higher-order slow rise/fall in Ras-GTP (does not go below zero)
function rs = RasHigherOrder(t, floor, scaleFactor)
val = (-(1e-5)*t^4 + (0.001)*t^3 - (0.0295)*t^2 + (0.3085)*t)/scaleFactor;
if (val <= floor)
rs = floor;
else
rs = val;
end
end
 
% Used for relatively fast oscillation of Ras-GTP input signal
oscilate = @(t, periodTerm, amplitude, phaseshift, vshift) amplitude*cos(periodTerm*t + phaseshift) + vshift;
 
% Local function to facillitate repeated solving for different Ras-GTP
% input signals over time
function solution = modulateRas(Rasfxn, ics, maxTime, allSpecies, params, tolerances)
ics(1) = Rasfxn(0); % Ras0 now determined by f(t)
f = @(t,x) CytosolicMAPK(t,x,params,true,Rasfxn);
[T2, X2] = ode15s(f, [0 maxTime], ics, tolerances);
Xtbl2 = array2table(X2, 'VariableNames', allSpecies);
Xtbl2.time = T2;
Xtbl2.Ras = arrayfun(Rasfxn, Xtbl2.time);
solution = Xtbl2;
end
 
% Try and plot solutions for:
% 1) A step up to 0.4 at t=1 and drop back to 0 at t=7
rs = @(t) RasStep(t,1,7,0,0.4);
tbl = modulateRas(rs, IC, 30, SpeciesNames, paramsTable, options);
plotResults(tbl, [0 30], [0 1], ["Ras", "ERK", "ERKp", "ERKpp"], ...
'ERK activation response to Ras-GTP step up/down', @lines, 'aftercolor');
 
% 2) A slower sweep up/down/up/down over ~60 minutes inspired by Figure 4
rs = @(t) RasHigherOrder(t, 0.000001, 5/1.5);
tbl = modulateRas(rs, IC, 80, SpeciesNames, paramsTable, options);
plotResults(tbl, [0 80], [0 1.5], ["Ras", "ERK", "ERKp", "ERKpp"], ...
'ERK activation response to Ras-GTP slower sweep up/down', @lines, 'aftercolor');
 
% 3) Faster regular oscillations across a wide range of Ras-GTP concentrations
rs = @(t) oscilate(t, 1, 1.4/2, -pi, 1.4/2);
tbl = modulateRas(rs, IC, 40, SpeciesNames, paramsTable, options);
plotResults(tbl, [0 40], [0 1.5], ["Ras", "ERK", "ERKp", "ERKpp"], ...
'ERK activation response to Ras-GTP fast large oscillations', @lines, 'aftercolor');
 
% 4) Fast oscillations across a smaller range of Ras-GTP concentrations
rs = @(t) oscilate(t, 1, 0.15/2, -pi, 0.15/2);
tbl = modulateRas(rs, IC, 40, SpeciesNames, paramsTable, options);
plotResults(tbl, [0 40], [0 1.5], ["Ras", "ERK", "ERKp", "ERKpp"], ...
'ERK activation response to Ras-GTP fast small oscillations', @lines, 'aftercolor');
 
%% Use the step function defined above for many different step-up
% concentrations of Ras-GTP to see what the effect on ERKpp dynamics are
 
% Local function facilliates changing the Ras step and plotting ERKpp
function RasERKppSensitivity(RasConcentrations, ylimit, ics, trangeMax, allspecies, params, tolerances)
figure
hold on
for i = 1:length(RasConcentrations)
rs = @(t) RasStep(t,1,30,0,RasConcentrations(i));
tbl = modulateRas(rs, ics, trangeMax, allspecies, params, tolerances);
plot(tbl.time,tbl.ERKpp);
end
set(gca,'FontSize',12);
ylabel('Concentration (μM)', FontSize=13);
xlabel('Time (minutes)', FontSize=13);
titleString = strcat("ERKpp response to varied Ras-GTP (", string(min(RasConcentrations)), " to ", string(max(RasConcentrations)), " by ", string(RasConcentrations(2)-RasConcentrations(1)), " μM)");
subtitleString = strcat("Step starts at t=1 and ends at t=30", " (Raf0=", string(ics(2)), " μM)");
title(titleString, FontSize=14);
subtitle(subtitleString, FontSize=13);
ylim([0 ylimit]);
hold off
end
 
%% Test a range of Ras-GTP step concentrations
RasSteps = 0.02:0.02:2;
RasERKppSensitivity(RasSteps, 0.35, IC, 50, SpeciesNames, paramsTable, options)
 
%% There is an upper limit on the steady state ERKpp concentration as
% the Ras-GTP concentration gets large, this seems to be strongly controlled
% by Raf0, all else remaining equal (parameters and initial conditions)
RasSteps = 0.02:0.02:2;
IC(2) = 0.03; % increase Raf0 from 0.013 to 0.03
RasERKppSensitivity(RasSteps, 0.35, IC, 50, SpeciesNames, paramsTable, options)
The plot above shows that conservation of mass holds in the solution of this system for a few key species, as the total concentration of any species containing each molecule type remains more or less constant near the initial value. There is some slight but more notable deviation in MEK and ERK from their initial concentrations. I think this can likely be explained by numerical error propagating through the system and affecting the final solution, and it seems unlikely to represent a major problem with the model's fidelity.
The top plot shows the solution for every species in the system in response to a constant Ras-GTP concentration equal to 0.4 μM (including complexes, which have "_" in their name). The colors are a little bit difficult to see clearly for some species, but it was difficult getting a combination of colormap and line type cycling that did not result in too many identical lines. Also, some of the smaller concentrations look constant at this scale, but do change with time (some, like [Ras], are effectively constant though). Because that first plot is a bit unweildy, instead of considering every species, I chose to focus on the big picture in the simulations and plots that follow by focusing on the effect of the input signal (Ras) on the final output response of ERKpp and its precursors. As such, the second plot is from the same solution, but only shows the Ras-GTP input and non-complex ERK species. One of the things that pop out in either of these plots is the relatively fast response time of the signaling pathway under these conditions/assumptions. It takes ERKpp less than 5 minutes to reach steady state concentration in response to a constant Ras concentration of 0.4 μM! While I know they were modeling a different and larger system, this timescale seems roughly consistent with what I could infer from the paper (e.g. from Figure 4) [1].

3) Exploring the model

Since these initial conditions are based on experimental data from other papers, and the model parameters were determined by fitting the model to proteomics data using the genetic algorithm, I think it is fair to assume this response timescale at least roughly reflects what is biologically possible [1]. While the cells, conditions, and experimental contexts vary, the response time seen above is more or less within the realm of what I could find reported in the literature for ERKpp or ERKp response to EGF signaling. For example, I found two papers that state (or show) that some form of phosphorylated ERK peaks by anywhere from 5 to 30 minutes after the EGF signal starts [5,6]. The model used here is, of course, fairly simplistic, because it does not include processes in the nucleus or the (I imagine numerous) other reactions in the cytosol that could impact this pathway's dynamics. That said, this model seems to at least capture an approximately realistic timescale for this part of EGF-MAPK signaling. As such, I decided to explore the system's reponse to various (admittedly not very natural) Ras-GTP stimuli below (code shown earlier):
Above on the left, I used a more formal step function to set Ras-GTP as a function of time (step starts at t=1 and ends at t=5). The response at the start of the step is the same as simply using Ras0=0.4 earlier, but it makes the change more visually explicit. On the other end, we get to see response in ERK activation to a sudden loss of signal, which is new. As noted before, it takes very little time (easily < 5 minutes) for ERKpp to reach its steady state concentration, but it takes much longer for all ERK to return to a deactivated state (e.g., ERKpp=0) after the signal is instantly and completely lost (not particularly biological). There is a little bit of "system memory" in the form of ERKp, which drifts downward even more slowly than ERKpp. I imagine this enables ERKpp to rebound more quickly for subsequent rises in the signal/Ras-GTP concentration. On the right, a slower but much larger swing in Ras concentration was used. While the y-axis scales on the left and right are not directly comparable, the plot on the right reveals that this system might not require as much as 0.4 μM of Ras-GTP to trigger a very similar steady state in ERKpp over a very similar timescale. It also shows that ERK activation is fairly insensitive to fairly dramatic swings in Ras-GTP concentration. The ERKpp concentration slowly shifts up a little bit, but not that much given the Ras concentrations that are reached in that second maximum. The same can be said about the dip downward after the first Ras maximum, as it affects ERKpp only weakly. It also seems worth noting that the solution shown on the right above is missing the characteristic "peak" of ERK activity (e.g. ERKpp) seen in this week's paper and elsewhere [1,6]. I think this is due to the absence the reactions in the nucleus, which would effectively degrade/"spend" this signal (see Figures 1 and 4 by Tian and Song) [1].
In the plots above, Ras concentration was oscillated fairly quickly for this system ("fast" may be an exhaggeration). On the left, the amplitude was larger, and on the right, it was smaller (all else remained the same). Consistent with what was seen earlier, ERKpp is fairly insensitive to relatively large swings in Ras-GTP concentration after the initial activation. In the vein of an observation made earlier, I think the slower response time of ERKp facillitates this relatively fast rebound in ERKpp after the Ras concentration reaches its minimum. What is interesting about the oscillations of Ras within a much lower concentration range is that they have a little bit more of an effect on ERKpp over time (right plot). In that plot, all ERK profiles are looking more "cosine-ish", even though the amplitudes of the oscilations are smaller than that of Ras. This implies that the ERKpp dynamics and steady state are a bit more sensitive to changes in Ras-GTP within a lower concentration range (although still not incredibly sensitive, all things considered).
The plots above got me wondering how sensitive the ERKpp response is to the Ras-GTP concentration. To take a brief look at this question, I repeatedly used the same step function from earlier varying the Ras concentrations for the step each time, over a range from 0.02 to 2 μM (in 0.02 μM increments). For this crude sensitivity test, the step began at t=1 minute but endded t=30, in order to give every system a large enough window for ERKpp to reach steady state before Ras dropped back to zero. This led to the plots below which, for visual clarity, only show the ERKpp responses (all code shown earlier):
The plot on the left was generated with the same Raf0 concentration from Table 1 by Tian and Song used so far (0.013 μM) [1]. The plot on the right was generating with Raf0 = 0.03 μM, but all other parameters and initial conditions were kept the same. This was done because I noticed that as the Ras-GTP step appproached relatively large concentrations, the steady state ERKpp concentration seemed to be approximately limited by Raf0. This makes sense, because Raf is the closest downstream transducer of the Ras signal, and its initial concentration is over an order of magnitude lower than all but the smallest Ras-GTP concentrations tested above. This ties in to what I think is the most intersting thing about the plots above, which is that the steady state ERKpp concentration, as well as the time it takes to reach that concentration, are most sensitive to changes occuring within the lower concentration regime for Ras-GTP. In either plot, each line corresponds to a Ras step concentration increase of 0.02 μM from the previous one. For the system from the paper (on the left), it can be seen that ERKpp sensitivity (somewhat arbitrarily) falls off after roughly the 10th ERKpp profile from the bottom, which corresponds to a 0.2 μM Ras-GTP step. This is consistent with the main observation made earlier for the more complex Ras-GTP profiles: for concentrations of Ras-GTP above some level, the ERKpp steady state (or approximately stable state) is fairly insensitive, even to dramatic changes in Ras-GTP.
Overall, the basic behavior observed in this model seems consistent with an important and robust external signal relay system (which, of course, EGF-MAPK is). Once the initial signal arrives via a sufficient spike in extracellular EGF and then cytsolic Ras-GTP, there are plently of reasons why it would be benefitial for final "ON" message (say, in the form of ERKpp) to remain relatively stable, at least solely with regard to what Ras-GTP does within a wide concentration range beyond that point. Of course, if Ras-GTP drops very low, then ERKpp does respond, albeit slowly and with a weak "memory" that it was recently activated (via a slower ERKp response). Taken together, in some contexts I imagine this could facillitate commiting to larger "decisions" within the cell based on the external signal, as we have seen a fairly fast and robust/consistent response to any sufficient level of Ras-GTP (and presumably EGF). This is coupled with a much slower return to the "OFF" state (i.e., ERKpp=0) and an abillity (via ERKp) to rebound more quickly for some time, even after the signal drops out completely. That said, ERKpp is to some extent more "modulatable" via swings within a lower concentation range of Ras-GTP (and presumably EGF), at least within this model. Whether or not this occurs or is useful in vivo is uncertain. Because I have been using analogies to electrical switches, it is worth nothing that this model is only concerned with the mechanism of the swtich itself, but not the complex circuitry it is connected to, or the motivation behind flipping it. Even so, it reveals some interesting things!

3) References
